locked_facts: Geostationary orbit: T = 24 hours, equatorial plane, circular, west to east
Geostationary height: h ≈ 35,786 km above Earth's surface (r ≈ 42,164 km from centre)
Geostationary satellite appears stationary from Earth — used for communication
Polar orbit: passes over both poles, covers entire Earth as it rotates beneath
Polar orbit height: typically 500-800 km, T ≈ 90-100 minutes
Only one geostationary orbit exists for any given planet (fixed r for T = 24 h)
truth_statements: A geostationary satellite has the same angular velocity as Earth and thus appears stationary from the ground
The geostationary orbit must be circular, in the equatorial plane, and at a specific height of about 36000 km
Polar satellites orbit at much lower altitudes and can scan the entire Earth over multiple orbits
The height of the geostationary orbit is determined solely by the requirement that T = 24 hours
Any satellite with T = 24 hours but not in the equatorial plane traces a figure-8 as seen from Earth (geosynchronous but not geostationary)

aha_moment.what_to_show: Earth rotating with a geostationary satellite above the equator — the satellite stays above the same point. A polar satellite zooms past much lower, covering different ground tracks each orbit as Earth rotates beneath it. Both serve different purposes.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Split view: geostationary satellite hovering over one point vs polar satellite tracing criss-cross paths over the globe
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think all satellites are 'in orbit' the same way — seeing the two fundamentally different orbit types and their purposes clarifies satellite design

simulation_states.label: Circular orbit basics
simulation_states.description: A satellite in circular orbit at height h. T calculated from Kepler's third law. Lower orbit = faster speed, shorter period.
simulation_states.emphasis: T increases with orbital radius: T² ∝ r³

simulation_states.label: Geostationary orbit
simulation_states.description: Set T = 24 hours → solve for r. The satellite must be at ~36,000 km in the equatorial plane going west to east. It hovers over one point on Earth.
simulation_states.emphasis: T = 24h uniquely determines the orbit radius — there's only one geostationary orbit

simulation_states.label: Geostationary vs polar
simulation_states.description: Geostationary satellite stays above one point (communication). Polar satellite at 800 km passes over poles, scanning different strips each orbit. Ground tracks shown on Earth map.
simulation_states.emphasis: Geostationary = always same point. Polar = scans entire Earth. Different heights, different jobs.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts orbital height. T and v computed. At h = 36,000 km, 'geostationary' label lights up. Ground track drawn on Earth map. Toggle between equatorial and polar inclination.
simulation_states.emphasis: Find the height where T = 24h. Compare with a low polar orbit.

common_misconceptions.correction: Geostationary satellites orbit at ~3 km/s — the same angular velocity as Earth's rotation. They APPEAR stationary from the ground because they match Earth's rotation exactly.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show satellite moving in orbit alongside Earth's rotation — both at the same angular speed

common_misconceptions.belief: Any satellite at the right height is geostationary
common_misconceptions.correction: Three conditions: (1) T = 24h (correct height), (2) orbit in equatorial plane, (3) circular orbit, west to east. A satellite at 36,000 km over the poles is NOT geostationary.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show same-height satellite in polar orbit — it's not stationary over any point
